context: Current closing price of ServiceNow (NOW): $87.79 USD as of 2026-04-14.
justification_text:
At the one-day horizon, my point estimate of 88.5 was anchored primarily to the most recent observed price or level, with short-term momentum and mean-reversion tendencies both considered. At this horizon, the dominant signal is simply continuity — prices or values rarely move dramatically in a single session absent a specific catalyst, so the estimate reflects a modest drift from wherever the series currently sits. The one-week estimate of 89.5 reflects a slight upward bias built into the underlying trend, perhaps from recent positive momentum or a modest structural drift, while the one-month estimate of 91.0 extends that same gradual upward trajectory further out, compounding the directional lean over a longer window.

The confidence interval widths expand meaningfully as the horizon lengthens, which reflects the classical square-root-of-time uncertainty scaling for financial or economic time series. At one day, the interval of 84–93 is relatively tight because there is simply less time for randomness to accumulate. By one week, the band opens to 80–99, incorporating the possibility of a meaningful trending move in either direction, multi-day momentum shifts, or a mid-week news event. At one month, the interval of 73–110 is quite wide, acknowledging that over four weeks, regime changes, macroeconomic data releases, geopolitical developments, or sentiment shifts can collectively push outcomes far from the starting point.

Specific risks that could push outcomes outside the stated intervals include sudden policy announcements — such as central bank decisions or fiscal policy surprises — that could gap prices sharply beyond the tails of the distribution. A major geopolitical shock, a financial contagion event, or an unexpected earnings or economic data release could cause single-session moves that breach even the one-day bounds. Liquidity crises or flash crashes represent tail risks that are structurally difficult to capture in symmetric intervals.

It is also worth noting that my intervals assume roughly stable volatility over each horizon, which itself is a source of model risk. If realized volatility spikes — as it tends to in stress periods — the true distribution would be considerably fatter-tailed than my stated ranges imply. Conversely, if conditions remain unusually calm, even the one-month interval may prove wider than necessary. The intervals should therefore be read as reasonable probabilistic bounds under normal-to-mildly-stressed conditions, not as hard guarantees against extreme outcomes.
